stem: A 5-month-old previously well infant is admitted with a 3-day history of coryza, cough, and progressively worsening work of breathing. She weighs 7 kg. Standard low-flow nasal oxygen at 2 L/min over the past 2 hours has been insufficient to keep saturations stable above 92 percent and feeds have stopped. She remains alert and the bedside nurse asks for your guidance on the next step. The respiratory consultant has been notified. She has no congenital heart disease.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168, RR 64, SpO2 90% on 2 L/min nasal prongs, Temp 37.6
data_table.Examination: Moderate intercostal and subcostal recession; tracheal tug; widespread expiratory wheeze with bilateral crackles; alert but tiring
data_table.Investigations: Clinical diagnosis; CXR not required routinely; nasopharyngeal swab pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence nebulised hypertonic saline 3 percent 4-hourly with 100 percent oxygen via Hudson mask
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline does not change duration of hospitalisation in bronchiolitis; rising oxygen requirement on low-flow nasal cannula indicates escalation to high-flow rather than nebuliser strategies. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg back to back and reassess response after the third dose has been administered
options.rationale: Salbutamol has no demonstrated benefit in bronchiolitis under 12 months and is not recommended; the appropriate response is escalation of oxygen support. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg into the lateral thigh and prepare for emergent intubation now
options.rationale: Intramuscular adrenaline is reserved for anaphylaxis, not bronchiolitis; intubation may be needed later but is not the immediate next step before non-invasive escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Start high-flow humidified nasal cannula oxygen at 2 L/kg/min FiO2 titrated to keep saturations above 92 percent
options.rationale: Heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min is the recommended escalation when low-flow oxygen fails to maintain saturations above 92 percent in moderate bronchiolitis; the PARIS trial demonstrated reduced escalation to ICU compared with standard oxygen. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days and continue current oxygen support without further escalation
options.rationale: Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in bronchiolitis under 12 months and should not be used routinely; rising oxygen requirement signals escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis)

explanation.summary: Bronchiolitis in infants is managed primarily with supportive care: hydration via nasogastric or intravenous route as needed and oxygen titrated to maintain saturations above 92 percent. When low-flow oxygen is insufficient, heated humidified high-flow nasal cannula at 2 L/kg/min reduces escalation to ICU compared with standard oxygen (PARIS trial). Salbutamol, steroids, hypertonic saline, and antibiotics are not routinely recommended.
key_points: Saturations target above 92 percent
HFNC 2 L/kg/min reduces ICU escalation (PARIS trial)
No role for salbutamol, steroids, antibiotics in typical bronchiolitis
Hydration: NG or IV when feeds dropping
explanation.pearls: PARIS trial: heated humidified HFNC at 2 L/kg/min reduces ICU escalation in infant bronchiolitis when low-flow oxygen fails; saturations target above 92 percent.

sources.label: PARIS trial - High-flow oxygen therapy in infants with bronchiolitis

stem:
A 5-year-old girl is brought to your general practice with 8 months of infrequent, hard, painful bowel motions every 5 to 7 days, soiling episodes 3 days per week with overflow incontinence, and intermittent abdominal pain. On examination her abdomen is soft with a palpable suprapubic faecal mass and her bottom is fissured externally. Her growth is on the 50th centile.

There are no red flags for organic disease and her parents are anxious about a recent school review. Toilet training was uneventful and there has been no recent stressor.

data_table.Examination: Palpable suprapubic faecal mass; perianal fissure visible; normal lower limb neurology; normal sacral spine; thyroid clinically normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Refer to paediatric surgery now for outpatient anorectal manometry and possible Hirschsprung biopsy work-up
options.rationale: Hirschsprung presents in infancy with delayed meconium and tight rectum on PR; a 5-year-old with classic functional constipation and overflow soiling does not require surgical work-up. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Recommend daily lactulose 5 mL after meals plus a high-fibre diet and weekly bowel diary review
options.rationale: Maintenance therapy without disimpaction in a child with palpable faecal loading risks ongoing overflow soiling and does not address the impacted faecal mass. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Prescribe stimulant senna 7.5 mg daily indefinitely with no disimpaction phase or behavioural toileting plan
options.rationale: Stimulant laxatives have a role as add-on therapy, but they should not be used as monotherapy in impacted constipation, and disimpaction with osmotic laxatives is the first step. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Schedule a sodium phosphate enema cycle weekly for 4 weeks in clinic before commencing oral therapy plans
options.rationale: Phosphate enemas carry hyperphosphataemia risk in children and are reserved for failed oral disimpaction; macrogol-based oral disimpaction is the standard first step. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Commence escalating macrogol disimpaction regimen over 3 to 7 days with maintenance dosing and behavioural toileting plan
options.rationale: Escalating macrogol (polyethylene glycol with electrolytes) is the recommended first-line disimpaction agent for paediatric functional constipation with faecal loading, with subsequent maintenance and behavioural toileting to prevent recurrence. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Paediatric functional constipation with faecal impaction requires disimpaction before maintenance. Escalating macrogol over 3 to 7 days is the first-line oral agent and avoids the risks of phosphate enemas. Maintenance dose macrogol is continued for months alongside scheduled toilet sits after meals, fibre and fluid optimisation, and reward systems. Stimulant laxatives are reserved for breakthrough. Red flags (delayed meconium, ribbon stools, sacral abnormality, faltering growth, abnormal lower limb neurology) warrant specialist work-up.
key_points: Disimpact first with escalating macrogol then maintain
Phosphate enemas carry hyperphosphataemia risk; avoid
Behavioural toileting after meals is essential
Red flags trigger specialist referral
explanation.pearls: Macrogol PEG with electrolytes escalating regimen is first-line disimpaction in paediatric functional constipation; do not skip disimpaction or overflow soiling will persist.

sources.label: NASPGHAN/ESPGHAN evidence-based recommendations on functional constipation

stem: A 28-year-old G1 woman at 38+5 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite reporting a clear gush of fluid 14 hours ago, with continued damp pads since. She is not contracting. Her group B streptococcus swab is negative and her antenatal course has been uncomplicated. The foetus is cephalic, the CTG is reassuring, and there are no signs of infection. She has been listening to her body and wonders if she should keep waiting or be induced. She has no contraindications to induction.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus; cephalic; pool of clear fluid in posterior fornix on speculum; no obvious meconium; foetal heart 140 reassuring
data_table.Investigations: GBS swab at 36 weeks negative; FBC unremarkable; CTG reassuring

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Expectant management with no specific time frame and reassessment only if maternal observations deteriorate further
options.rationale: Open-ended expectant management without a defined plan increases chorioamnionitis risk; RANZCOG suggests offering induction by 24 hours for term PROM. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Immediate category 2 caesarean section because of duration of ruptured membranes exceeding the 12-hour threshold
options.rationale: Caesarean is not indicated for term PROM with reassuring foetal status, cephalic presentation, and no contraindication to induction. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Discharge home to await spontaneous labour with planned return in 72 hours regardless of new symptoms or labour status
options.rationale: Discharge with no observation plan misses early infection and exceeds the safe expectant window in term PROM; review at minimum every 12 hours is required if expectant. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Offer induction now with oxytocin titration; continue maternal and foetal monitoring, and antibiotics if not in labour by 24 hours
options.rationale: RANZCOG advises offering induction at term PROM rather than open-ended expectant management because immediate induction reduces chorioamnionitis without increasing caesarean rate; intrapartum antibiotics are added if not in labour by 18 to 24 hours. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Vaginal misoprostol 50 micrograms every 4 hours up to 6 doses for cervical priming despite reassuring foetal status
options.rationale: Vaginal prostaglandin in unfavourable cervix is one option, but high-dose misoprostol regimens are not the RANZCOG default and oxytocin induction is appropriate first line for PROM. (Source: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

explanation.summary: Term PROM is managed with offer of induction rather than open-ended expectant management because immediate induction reduces chorioamnionitis without increasing caesarean rate (TERMPROM trial). Oxytocin is the typical induction agent in PROM. Intrapartum antibiotics (benzylpenicillin or clindamycin if allergic) are added if labour has not commenced by 18 to 24 hours, or sooner if GBS positive. Expectant management requires defined monitoring intervals.
key_points: Offer induction at term PROM (TERMPROM trial)
Oxytocin is first-line induction in PROM
Antibiotics if not labouring by 18 to 24 hours
Reduced chorioamnionitis without increased caesarean
explanation.pearls: Term PROM: offer induction with oxytocin and add intrapartum antibiotics if labour not established by 18 to 24 hours; TERMPROM trial supports immediate over expectant management.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

subtopic_detail: Endometriosis stepwise treatment after first-line failure
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to a gynaecology clinic with 4 years of progressive dysmenorrhoea, deep dyspareunia, and chronic non-cyclical pelvic pain. She has tried 9 months of combined oral contraceptive pill continuous regimen with partial relief, and 6 months of NSAIDs around her period, with continued symptoms restricting work. A recent specialised pelvic ultrasound demonstrates a 3 cm right ovarian endometrioma and posterior compartment deep infiltrating endometriosis. She wishes to maintain fertility options for future pregnancy and is uncertain about surgery. The MDT has reviewed her case.

data_table.Examination: Tender posterior fornix; right adnexal tenderness; nodularity at uterosacral palpation
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: 3 cm right endometrioma, kissing ovaries pattern, posterior DIE; AMH 19 pmol/L; pregnancy test negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Switch to depot medroxyprogesterone acetate 150 mg intramuscular every 12 weeks as long-term suppression
options.rationale: DMPA can suppress symptoms but causes bone density loss and a delay to return of fertility of up to 9 months; it is not the preferred next-line for women planning future pregnancy with deep disease. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Begin tamoxifen 20 mg daily as an alternative endocrine modulator targeting endometriotic implants and pain reduction
options.rationale: Tamoxifen is not used for endometriosis; in fact it has been associated with endometrial pathology and is the wrong drug class for this indication. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Refer to assisted reproduction for in vitro fertilisation now without further suppression or symptom-directed therapy
options.rationale: Fertility-focused IVF is appropriate when conception is the immediate goal; she wishes to preserve fertility but is not currently trying to conceive, so symptom management remains primary. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Proceed to laparoscopic excision of endometriosis and ovarian cystectomy after detailed informed consent and fertility counselling
options.rationale: After medical therapy failure with deep infiltrating endometriosis and an endometrioma over 3 cm causing persistent symptoms, laparoscopic excision is recommended; cystectomy is preferred over ablation for endometrioma recurrence reduction. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Commence a 6-month GnRH agonist trial with bone-protective add-back tibolone and reassess pain on completion
options.rationale: GnRH agonists with add-back can suppress symptoms temporarily, but with deep disease and an endometrioma over 3 cm not improving on combined oral contraceptive, surgery is the recommended next step. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis Guideline)

explanation.summary: Endometriosis with failure of first-line hormonal and NSAID therapy and clinically significant disease (deep infiltrating disease, endometrioma over 3 cm with persistent symptoms) is escalated to laparoscopic excisional surgery in centres with appropriate expertise. Cystectomy is preferred over ablation for endometriomas because of lower recurrence. GnRH agonist trials are options when surgery is declined or deferred but do not replace it for advanced disease. Future fertility is discussed pre-operatively.
key_points: Surgery indicated when medical therapy fails or DIE/endometrioma over 3 cm
Laparoscopic excision and cystectomy preferred
Cystectomy reduces endometrioma recurrence vs ablation
Fertility counselling before surgery essential
explanation.pearls: Endometriosis pathway: COCP/NSAID first, GnRH agonist if surgery deferred, surgery (laparoscopic excision + cystectomy) for DIE or endometrioma over 3 cm with persistent symptoms.

stem: A 58-year-old woman presents to her general practice with 4 days of left calf swelling, pain, and tenderness. She returned from a 12-hour flight from Singapore 6 days ago. She has a history of stage II breast cancer 18 months ago treated with lumpectomy and tamoxifen, but no active disease. Her left calf is 4.5 cm larger than her right and she has pitting oedema. There is no evidence of an alternative diagnosis on examination. She is haemodynamically stable and walks unaided. She has no history of bleeding.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84, BP 124/76, RR 16, SpO2 98% RA, Temp 36.8
data_table.Examination: Left calf 4.5 cm > right; pitting oedema; tenderness along deep venous system; superficial veins not prominent; no cellulitis
data_table.Investigations: FBC and renal function normal; D-dimer pending

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange D-dimer first and proceed to ultrasound only if positive without calculating a Wells probability score
options.rationale: D-dimer is unreliable as a standalone test; the recommended pathway uses risk stratification first then D-dimer or ultrasound depending on probability. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - VTE)

options.text: Refer immediately for inferior vena cava filter placement to prevent pulmonary embolism while diagnosis is being confirmed
options.rationale: IVC filters are reserved for confirmed VTE with anticoagulation contraindication or breakthrough VTE on anticoagulation; they are not used presumptively. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - VTE)

options.text: Discharge with leg elevation and a compression stocking and review in 1 week if symptoms have not resolved fully
options.rationale: A patient with multiple risk factors and clinical signs of DVT must not be discharged without proceeding through the diagnostic pathway; missed DVT carries PE risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - VTE)

options.text: Calculate Wells score; if DVT likely, arrange same-day proximal compression ultrasound and consider therapeutic anticoagulation pending result
options.rationale: With recent immobility, active cancer history, calf swelling over 3 cm, pitting oedema, and tenderness, Wells score is high; the pathway is direct compression ultrasound rather than D-dimer because high pre-test probability undermines D-dimer specificity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - VTE)

options.text: Commence empirical oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for presumed cellulitis given calf erythema and warmth
options.rationale: Calf swelling with tenderness in a high-risk patient is DVT until proven otherwise; antibiotics for presumed cellulitis without erythema typical of infection delays appropriate investigation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - VTE)

explanation.summary: The 2-level Wells score for DVT stratifies into likely (2 or more) or unlikely (1 or less). DVT-likely patients proceed to proximal compression ultrasound. DVT-unlikely patients have a D-dimer; positive D-dimer triggers ultrasound. Therapeutic anticoagulation is started pending ultrasound when delay is expected and bleeding risk is acceptable. Confirmed proximal DVT is treated with anticoagulation for at least 3 months, with extended duration if unprovoked or cancer-associated.
key_points: Wells 2+ goes to ultrasound directly
Wells under 2 uses D-dimer first
Cancer or active malignancy scores 1
Empirical anticoagulation if delay to scan and acceptable bleeding risk
explanation.pearls: DVT pathway: calculate 2-level Wells - likely (2+) goes to direct USS, unlikely uses D-dimer; high pre-test probability invalidates D-dimer.

stem: A 48-year-old woman attends her general practice for a routine review. Three separate in-clinic blood pressure readings over 6 weeks have been 152/94, 148/92, and 156/96 using a calibrated upper-arm device. She is asymptomatic, has no diabetes, and has no end-organ damage on initial screening. Her lipids and ECG are unremarkable. Her absolute cardiovascular risk is calculated as moderate. She is keen to know whether she has hypertension that warrants pharmacotherapy or whether further confirmation is appropriate before treatment.

data_table.Examination: No end-organ examination findings; cardiovascular and respiratory examination unremarkable
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm with no LVH criteria; eGFR 92 mL/min; urinary ACR 1.2 mg/mmol; lipids HDL 1.3, LDL 3.4, TG 1.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Start perindopril 4 mg daily today based on consistent in-clinic readings above the threshold for stage 1 hypertension
options.rationale: NHF guidance recommends out-of-office confirmation by ABPM or HBPM before commencing pharmacotherapy in most cases to exclude white-coat effect, unless severely elevated or evidence of end-organ damage. (Source: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016

options.text: Repeat clinic blood pressure measurement in 12 months without confirmatory home or ambulatory blood pressure assessment
options.rationale: Persistent elevated clinic readings require timely confirmation; deferring assessment 12 months risks ongoing exposure to elevated pressures if true hypertension. (Source: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016

options.text: Arrange polysomnography for obstructive sleep apnoea screening before any further diagnostic blood pressure measurement
options.rationale: OSA assessment is appropriate when there are specific signs or refractory hypertension; it is not the first step in confirming a hypertension diagnosis. (Source: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016

options.text: Refer to endocrinology for plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio testing to exclude primary hyperaldosteronism before treatment
options.rationale: Secondary hypertension work-up is indicated in resistant, severe, or early-onset disease or with suggestive features; it is not first-line for moderate sustained hypertension. (Source: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016

options.text: Arrange 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring or 7-day home BP averages, treating if daytime average above 135/85
options.rationale: NHF Australia recommends ABPM or HBPM to confirm hypertension before pharmacotherapy in most patients; the diagnostic threshold is daytime average over 135/85 (ABPM/HBPM), which corresponds to clinic over 140/90. (Source: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016

explanation.summary: Australian hypertension diagnosis requires confirmation of sustained elevation, typically by ambulatory or home blood pressure monitoring before commencing treatment, except in severely elevated readings or when end-organ damage is present. Diagnostic thresholds: clinic over 140/90, ABPM daytime average over 135/85, HBPM 7-day average over 135/85. Treatment decisions also account for absolute cardiovascular risk and target lower BP thresholds in higher-risk groups.
key_points: Confirm with ABPM or HBPM before treating
ABPM/HBPM threshold 135/85; clinic 140/90
Absolute CV risk drives treatment decisions
Treat immediately if severely elevated or end-organ damage
explanation.pearls: NHF Australia: confirm hypertension with ABPM (daytime average over 135/85) or 7-day HBPM averages over 135/85 before treating; clinic 140/90 alone is insufficient unless severe or end-organ damage.

sources.label: National Heart Foundation Australia Hypertension Guideline 2016

stem: A 67-year-old man presents with 2 weeks of progressive dyspnoea and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and recently lost 5 kg unintentionally. Chest x-ray shows a large right pleural effusion and CT chest demonstrates pleural thickening with no obvious mass. Diagnostic thoracentesis is performed. The serum LDH is 220 U/L (upper limit of normal 240) and serum protein 68 g/L. The pleural fluid analysis is shown. He is afebrile.

data_table.Examination: Right basal stony dullness; reduced breath sounds; no consolidation; no peripheral oedema; JVP not raised
data_table.Investigations: Pleural fluid: protein 38 g/L, LDH 220 U/L, glucose 4.2 mmol/L, pH 7.36; cytology pending; AFB and culture sent. Serum LDH 220 U/L (ULN 240), serum protein 68 g/L

lead_in: How is this pleural effusion best classified using Light's criteria?

options.text: Transudate because the pleural fluid LDH is below the upper limit of normal serum LDH despite raised protein
options.rationale: Light's criteria require only ONE positive criterion to label exudate; the pleural protein to serum protein ratio above 0.5 already meets criteria, making this an exudate. (Source: Light's Pleural Effusion Criteria)

options.text: Transudate consistent with cardiac failure because the patient has a smoking history and dyspnoea on exertion
options.rationale: Clinical impression cannot override Light's biochemical criteria; pleural protein to serum protein ratio of 0.56 categorically meets exudate criteria. (Source: Light's Pleural Effusion Criteria)

options.text: Exudate, but only if pleural fluid LDH exceeds two thirds of the upper limit of normal serum LDH for laboratory comparison
options.rationale: Pleural LDH above two thirds of ULN serum is just one of three criteria; this case meets the protein ratio criterion regardless of whether the LDH criteria is met. (Source: Light's Pleural Effusion Criteria)

options.text: Transudate based on the pleural fluid to serum LDH ratio being below 0.6 and otherwise unremarkable biochemistry
options.rationale: Light's criteria use ANY one of three positive results to label exudate; the protein ratio criterion is met (0.56 over 0.5), making this an exudate irrespective of the LDH ratio. (Source: Light's Pleural Effusion Criteria)

options.text: Exudate because the pleural fluid to serum protein ratio exceeds 0.5, requiring investigation for cause including malignancy
options.rationale: Light's criteria: ANY one of pleural protein/serum protein over 0.5, pleural LDH/serum LDH over 0.6, or pleural LDH over two thirds of ULN serum LDH defines exudate; protein ratio 38/68 = 0.56 meets criteria. (Source: Light's Pleural Effusion Criteria)

explanation.summary: Light's criteria classify a pleural effusion as exudative if any one of the following is positive: pleural protein to serum protein ratio over 0.5, pleural LDH to serum LDH ratio over 0.6, or pleural LDH greater than two thirds of upper limit of normal serum LDH. Exudates require workup for causes including malignancy, infection, pulmonary embolism, and connective tissue disease. Pleural fluid cytology, microbiology, and imaging guide further evaluation; thoracoscopy may be needed when cytology is non-diagnostic.
key_points: ANY one positive criterion = exudate
Protein ratio over 0.5 alone is sufficient
Exudates: malignancy, infection, PE, connective tissue disease
Cytology, microbiology, thoracoscopy as needed for cause
explanation.pearls: Light's criteria: ANY one of pleural/serum protein over 0.5, pleural/serum LDH over 0.6, or pleural LDH greater than two thirds ULN serum LDH defines exudate.

stem: A 24-year-old woman is referred to community mental health by her general practitioner after her third presentation in 6 months with non-suicidal self-harm and emotional dysregulation. She meets DSM-5-TR criteria for borderline personality disorder, with affective instability, intense interpersonal relationships, identity disturbance, and recurrent suicidal gestures. She is not currently psychotic, not in a major depressive episode, and is not using substances problematically. She is motivated to engage with structured treatment and has stable housing. She works part-time.

data_table.Examination: Linear superficial cuts on forearm; reactive mood; intact insight; no acute psychotic features; no suicidal intent today
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods and TSH normal; urine drug screen negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line intervention for the disorder itself?

options.text: Long-term hospitalisation in a specialist personality disorder inpatient unit for 6 to 12 months of intensive containment
options.rationale: Prolonged inpatient admission for BPD reinforces dependency and undermines self-regulation skills; community-based structured psychotherapy is the first-line. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

options.text: Lifelong second-generation antipsychotic such as quetiapine 200 mg nightly as the primary therapeutic intervention
options.rationale: There is no medication approved as first-line monotherapy for BPD; medications are used adjunctively for specific co-morbidities. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

options.text: Brief solution-focused cognitive behavioural therapy over 6 weeks targeted only at self-harm reduction at presentation
options.rationale: Brief CBT is insufficient for BPD; structured longer-term therapies such as DBT, mentalisation-based therapy, or schema therapy have evidence for sustained improvement. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

options.text: Structured psychological therapy such as dialectical behaviour therapy for at least 12 months with case management support
options.rationale: The NHMRC BPD guideline recommends structured psychological therapy (DBT, MBT, schema therapy, or transference-focused psychotherapy) for at least 12 months as the first-line treatment, with crisis planning and case management. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

options.text: Discharge to primary care with crisis line contact details and an SSRI prescription for emotional dysregulation
options.rationale: Discharge without structured therapy misses the evidence-based intervention; SSRIs are not specifically effective for BPD as monotherapy. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder is best treated with structured psychological therapy delivered over at least 12 months: dialectical behaviour therapy, mentalisation-based therapy, schema therapy, or transference-focused psychotherapy. Medication is adjunctive for co-morbid depression, anxiety, or psychotic-like symptoms but not first-line monotherapy. Brief admissions for safety are acceptable; long admissions are counterproductive. Crisis planning, written safety plans, and consistent case management are foundational.
key_points: Structured psychotherapy at least 12 months is first-line
DBT, MBT, schema, or TFP are evidence-based
Medications adjunctive only
Avoid prolonged inpatient stays
explanation.pearls: NHMRC BPD guideline: structured psychotherapy (DBT/MBT/schema/TFP) for at least 12 months is the first-line; long admissions worsen outcomes through dependency.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

subtopic_detail: Antipsychotic metabolic monitoring at baseline and 12 weeks
stem: A 27-year-old man with first-episode schizophrenia was commenced on olanzapine 10 mg nocte 12 weeks ago. He has had a good clinical response with reduction in positive symptoms and minimal extrapyramidal effects. He attends today for the 12-week metabolic review. His baseline measurements showed normal weight, fasting glucose 4.8, lipids unremarkable, and BP 118/72. He has gained 6 kg since commencing treatment. He has no family history of diabetes or premature cardiovascular disease and has otherwise stayed well.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78, BP 134/88, weight 86 kg (baseline 80), waist 102 cm
data_table.Examination: No EPS; mental state stable; no acute distress; no acanthosis nigricans
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 5.8 mmol/L (baseline 4.8), HbA1c 5.6 percent, total cholesterol 5.4 mmol/L, LDL 3.5, HDL 1.1, TG 2.4, ECG QTc 442 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue olanzapine unchanged and rely on routine 12 monthly reviews because no parameter exceeds a threshold for action
options.rationale: Multiple metabolic parameters have moved adversely (weight, waist circumference, fasting glucose, lipids, BP), reflecting evolving metabolic syndrome; intervention rather than annual review is needed. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Metabolic Monitoring)

options.text: Add metformin 500 mg twice daily empirically and continue olanzapine without further behavioural or dietary intervention
options.rationale: Metformin can mitigate antipsychotic weight gain but is added alongside diet, exercise, and consideration of antipsychotic switch where appropriate, not as the sole intervention. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Metabolic Monitoring)

options.text: Cease olanzapine abruptly and start ziprasidone 80 mg twice daily today as a metabolically neutral antipsychotic option
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation with cross-taper to ziprasidone, which has QTc concerns and is not currently PBS-listed in Australia, is not appropriate as the immediate response. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Metabolic Monitoring)

options.text: Stop all antipsychotic therapy because metabolic adverse effects outweigh symptomatic response in a first episode case
options.rationale: First-episode psychosis benefits from at least 12 to 24 months of maintenance treatment to reduce relapse; abandonment of antipsychotic therapy after metabolic concerns alone is inappropriate. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Metabolic Monitoring)

options.text: Implement lifestyle intervention, consider switch to aripiprazole given metabolic trajectory, and arrange repeat metabolic panel in 12 weeks
options.rationale: RANZCP recommends combining lifestyle intervention with consideration of switching to a less weight-and-glucose-adverse antipsychotic such as aripiprazole when metabolic deterioration develops; reassessment within 12 weeks confirms response. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Metabolic Monitoring)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic metabolic monitoring requires baseline weight, waist circumference, fasting glucose, lipids, blood pressure, and ECG. Reassessment at 6 and 12 weeks then 3-monthly to 1 year is recommended for second-generation agents, with annual review thereafter if stable. Deterioration prompts lifestyle intervention, consideration of switching to less metabolically active agents (such as aripiprazole), and adjuncts such as metformin where appropriate. Continued treatment is essential in first-episode psychosis.
key_points: Baseline + 6, 12 week, 3-monthly to 1 year, then annual
Aripiprazole less weight/glucose adverse than olanzapine
Metformin adjunct can mitigate weight gain
Lifestyle intervention always added
explanation.pearls: Olanzapine carries highest metabolic burden of second-generation antipsychotics; switching to aripiprazole when deterioration occurs improves the metabolic trajectory while maintaining psychosis treatment.

stem: A 31-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 4 years. She presents to your community psychiatry clinic having recently entered a new relationship and wishes to consider pregnancy in the next 12 to 24 months. She has been counselled previously about valproate risks but asks for a detailed plan. Her mood has remained euthymic with no episodes for 3 years on valproate monotherapy. She is otherwise well, takes no other medications, and has no contraindications to alternative mood stabilisers. She has no children.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic mental state; no tremor; full insight; capacity preserved
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 540 micromol/L; LFTs normal; FBC normal; pregnancy test negative; folate level adequate

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue valproate at the current dose with prescribed high-dose folate 5 mg daily for 3 months before conception
options.rationale: Valproate causes neural tube defects in around 10 percent and cognitive impairment in around 30 to 40 percent of exposed offspring; folate supplementation does not mitigate these risks. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Reduce valproate to 500 mg daily to lessen teratogenic exposure during planned conception while maintaining mood stability
options.rationale: Teratogenicity is dose-related but no dose is considered safe in early pregnancy; reducing dose risks relapse and does not eliminate teratogenicity. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Switch to carbamazepine 400 mg twice daily as an alternative mood stabiliser with comparable mood-stabilising properties
options.rationale: Carbamazepine is also teratogenic (around 3 percent neural tube defects, hand abnormalities, and cardiac defects) and is not the preferred substitute in women planning pregnancy. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Cross-taper to a safer mood stabiliser such as lamotrigine, document highly effective contraception throughout, and refer to perinatal psychiatry
options.rationale: Australian TGA pregnancy-prevention guidance for valproate requires highly effective contraception in women of childbearing potential, transition to a safer mood stabiliser such as lamotrigine before conception, and specialist perinatal psychiatric input. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Stop valproate today without substitution and rely on psychological therapy alone until pregnancy is confirmed
options.rationale: Untreated bipolar disorder during planned conception risks severe relapse with associated maternal and foetal harm; substitution rather than monotherapy cessation is required. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is associated with around 10 percent neural tube defects, cleft palate, hypospadias, and 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental impairment in exposed offspring. TGA guidance restricts valproate prescription in women of childbearing potential to those for whom no alternative is appropriate, mandates highly effective contraception (LARC or sterilisation), and requires annual review with risk acknowledgement. Cross-taper to lamotrigine or low-dose lithium are preferred when planning pregnancy, with perinatal psychiatry support.
key_points: Valproate teratogenicity: ~10 percent major malformation, 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental
TGA pregnancy prevention programme mandatory in women of childbearing potential
Switch to lamotrigine or low-dose lithium before conception
Perinatal psychiatry referral for planning
explanation.pearls: Valproate in pregnancy: 10 percent major malformation and 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental harm; TGA mandates highly effective contraception and transition to lamotrigine before planned conception.
